data.title: My husband sold his IRA without my knowledge prior to our divorce. Is this legal?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Is it legal for one member of a married couple to authorize the sale of an IRA in a joint-managed account?'

"Did the wealth-management company have a fiduciary responsibility to notify me prior to the sale?" (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

My now ex-husband sold most of his remaining IRA without my knowledge or approval prior to our divorce. Is it legal for one member of a married couple to authorize the sale of an IRA in a joint-managed account without the other member being notified or giving approval for its sale in the state of Michigan?

Did the wealth-management company have a fiduciary responsibility to notify me prior to the sale?

Feeling Swindled

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Don't miss: 'This scam stuff is going to get worse': A man approached me in my car - he had a crazy story

"Does the adviser or wealth manager have a fiduciary duty to you? No. Would the courts support your claim of dissipation of assets? Perhaps."

Dear Feeling,

Sometimes there's a fine line between "swindled" and "hoodwinked." In the former situation, you may have more recourse than in the latter.

There are two separate issues here: 1) the wealth manager's duty to you and to your husband, and 2) whether your husband breached orders laid down by the divorce court. It's more likely that he could have done the latter, if these transfers were made during your divorce proceedings and contravened the orders of a divorce court.

Your ex-husband's financial adviser and wealth-management company have a fiduciary duty to act in his best interests and put his requirements above their own. He is the client. He is the owner of the account. He is the one calling the shots. They take instruction from your ex-husband and have no obligation to his current or former wife.

There are possible exceptions based on fiduciary rules and legal rules. 1) If you were both clients of the same adviser or wealth manager, they may also have a fiduciary duty to you to keep you informed. 2) If there was a financial restraining order or injunction in place at the time your husband emptied or transferred the IRA to another account, that action would breach those rules.

Michigan is an equitable-distribution state. Unlike a community-property state, assets are not divided 50/50. Instead, they are divided equitably. That applies to retirement accounts. Does the adviser or wealth manager have a fiduciary duty to you? No. Would the courts support your claim of dissipation of assets? Perhaps.

"In Michigan, retirement accounts, such as 401(k)s, traditional/Roth IRAs, and pensions, can qualify as marital property subject to division if a spouse opened the account during their marriage," James Kraayeveld, a family-law attorney in Grand Rapids, Mich., writes in this guide on retirement accounts.

Growth of a retirement account

"However, even if a spouse had a retirement account before getting married, any contributions or growth in value of the account that accumulates during the marriage may constitute marital property," he adds. "When a spouse opens a retirement account during marriage, the court may divide the entire account during divorce."

But laws vary by state. In Michigan, IRAs can be split during a divorce, but tax-free transfers are subject to certain conditions, says Lucido & Manzella, a law firm in Clinton Township, Mich. "The IRA transfer is provided for in the divorce decree or property settlement agreement. The funds are transferred directly from one spouse's IRA to the other spouse's IRA."

"If an IRA is moved without following these rules exactly, the IRA holder will owe federal income taxes plus a 10% penalty on the transferred amount if under the age of 591/2," the law firm says. "This can create major financial implications and stress. Thus, it is vital to ensure that an IRA is properly divided in a divorce proceeding."

Lucido & Manzella continues: "Retirement savings such as 401(k) plans, pensions, IRAs and similar accounts are considered marital property and are subject to division during divorce proceedings in Michigan. It is important to note that only the portion of retirement savings that accumulated during the marriage is typically subject to division."

Rules regarding 401(k)s are set by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act and subject to spousal-consent rules. This act does not apply to IRAs, although state laws - including those in Michigan - may differ. Erisa is a federal law that sets minimum standards for most voluntary retirement and health plans.

Your next call should be to your divorce attorney to sort out the timeline of events that led to your husband liquidating his IRA.

Related: 'I have a great mortgage rate': I need $80K to buy my husband out of our home. Do I raid my $180K Roth IRA?

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'She lives alone': My mother-in-law, 86, gets $1,300 in Social Security. Is that enough to live on?

'I'm scared to lose': I have $140K in a 401(k) and earn $45K a year. Can I retire in 20 years?

My late husband's employer is forcing me to take 10% 401(k) distributions. Help!

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-13-25 0829ET

data.title: Donald Trump Jr.'s new career: Advising companies
data.description:
By James Rogers

From mixed martial arts to prediction markets and drones, the president's eldest son is being paid for giving his counsel

Donald Trump Jr. prepares to board Air Force One in Scotland this summer.

Donald Trump Jr.'s insights and expertise are in demand across a range of ambitious but little-known companies. He's now advising businesses with operations spanning mixed martial arts, prediction markets, investment banking, drone manufacturing, gun sales and online marketplaces.

Since his father won the presidential election in November 2024, Trump Jr. has become an adviser to eight companies. The 47-year-old is the long-serving executive vice president of the Trump Organization, where he oversees its real-estate property portfolio, and also sits on the board of Trump Media & Technology Group Corp. (DJT), which operates the president's social-media platform Truth Social.

In the past 10 months, Trump Jr. has joined corporate advisory boards and struck agreements around becoming a consultant or strategic advisor to companies.

On Nov. 27, Trump Jr. joined the advisory board of Unusual Machines Inc. (UMAC), a drone and drone-component maker based in Orlando, Fla. A Securities & Exchange Commission filing by Unusual Machines the day Trump Jr.'s role was announced showed that he owned 332,000 shares in the company. The company's stock soared by 85% the day after his role was unveiled.

"Don Jr. joining our board of advisors provides us unique expertise we need as we bring drone-component manufacturing back to America," said Unusual Machines Chief Executive Allan Evans at the time. The company did not respond to a request for comment.

A month later, in December 2024, Trump Jr. became a consultant for GrabAGun Digital Holdings Inc. (PEW), a gun seller based in Coppell, Texas. In return, he received 300,000 shares of GrabAGun, an SEC filing shows. GrabAGun listed its stock on the New York Stock Exchange in a July IPO that priced at $21.40 per share, valuing Trump Jr.'s stock at $6.4 million. He also joined the company's board that month.

"Donald J. Trump Jr. brings a deep understanding of the firearms industry," GrabAGun said in an SEC filing. "Our business may be harmed, in particular, if Donald J. Trump Jr. ceases to be involved with GrabAGun." In a statement, a GrabAGun spokesperson said Trump Jr. "is working to drive growth for the company while preserving and protecting our fundamental rights as Americans."

Trump Jr.'s advisory work has also been connected to his role at 1789 Capital, a venture-capital firm he joined as a partner after the 2024 presidential election. In August, Polymarket, the world's biggest prediction market, announced that Trump Jr. had joined the company as a strategic adviser as part of an investment that 1789 Capital made in Polymarket. Terms of the investment were not disclosed.

Days after the announcement, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission issued a letter of no action allowing Polymarket to relaunch its services in the U.S. The CFTC had in 2022 prohibited Polymarket from serving U.S.-based customers.

Donald Trump Jr., the Trump Organization, 1789 Capital and Polymarket did not respond to requests for comment.

The Company He Keeps: Donald Trump Jr.'s new advisory relationships 

   Company                          Role                     Start Date 
   Mixed Martial Arts Group         Strategic Advisor        Sept. 9, 2025 
   Polymarket                       Advisory Board Member    Aug. 26, 2025 
   New America Acquisition I Corp.  Advisory Board Member    May 28, 2025 
   Dominari Holdings                Advisory Board Member    Feb. 11, 2025 
   Kalshi                           Strategic Advisor        Jan. 12, 2025 
   GrabAGun                         Consultant and Director  Dec. 31, 2024 
   PSQ Holdings                     Director                 Dec. 3, 2024 
   Unusual Machines                 Advisory Board Member    Nov. 27, 2024

Jordan Libowitz, vice president of watchdog group Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington, noted Trump Jr.'s prior career experience predominantly revolved around the Trump Organization and related family businesses.

"Something really important is that there's a ton of these [business relationships] now, and there's no history of him being in these areas before," said Libowitz. "His history has always been in his father's companies, mostly in his father's real-estate business.

"You have to ask whether he is being added to these boards in an attempt to get these companies ahead with the Trump administration," Libowitz added.

In the past, Trump Jr. has had engagements outside the Trump family businesses, but they were relatively sparse. For example, he joined publisher Cambridge Who's Who as executive director of global branding and networking in 2010. The New York Times reported that he worked with the company for about a year.

During the Biden administration, congressional and media scrutiny focused on the international business interests of Joe Biden's son, Hunter Biden, over the period when his father was U.S. vice president and immediately following. Payments Hunter Biden received while he was a director at Ukrainian energy company Burisma and business ties to companies in countries like Romania and China became the subject of congressional reports and were criticized by Trump.

In his latest move, Trump Jr. teamed up with Mixed Martial Arts Group Ltd. (MMA), which announced this week that he is joining as a strategic adviser. As part of Trump Jr.'s two-year contract with Mixed Martial Arts Group, a company affiliated with him received 1.5 million Mixed Martial Arts Group stock options that are exercisable for $.001 per share, an SEC filing shows. Those options were recently valued at $2 million. Trump Jr. cannot sell those shares for one year, according to the securities filing.

Mixed Martial Arts Group did not respond to a request for comment.

Trump Jr. and his brother, Eric Trump, also this year joined the advisory board of investment bank Dominari Holdings Inc. (DOMH) and participated in a private placement in the company.

As part of the private placement, Trump Jr. purchased 216,138 shares in Dominari and warrants to purchase up to 432,276 shares, for an aggregate purchase price of $1 million. Dominari also issued Trump Jr. 250,000 shares upon his appointment to the company's advisory board, according to a securities filing. Dominari pointed MarketWatch to its SEC filings when asked to comment.

In a separate filing in April, Dominari said that Trump Jr. had amassed 1,648,414 shares in the company, but sold 1,504,322, leaving him with 144,092 shares. As of Friday's closing price, Trump Jr.'s stake was worth $945,244.

-James Rogers

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-13-25 0830ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Xinhua Silk Road: 2025 CIFTIS builds open cooperation platform, injecting new impetus into global trade in services
data.description:
Xinhua Silk Road: 2025 CIFTIS builds open cooperation platform, injecting new impetus into global trade in services

Xinhua Silk Road: 2025 CIFTIS builds open cooperation platform, injecting new impetus into global trade in services

PR Newswire

BEIJING, Sept. 13, 2025

BEIJING, Sept. 13, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- The 2025 China International Fair for Trade in Services (CIFTIS) opened in Beijing on September 10, under the theme "Embrace Intelligent Technologies, Empower Trade in Services". 

This year's CIFTIS attracts nearly 500 Fortune Global 500 companies and industry-leading enterprises. Participants come from 26 of the world's top 30 countries and regions in terms of services trade, with an international participation rate exceeding 20 percent.

Highlights of the 2025 CIFTIS include joint displays by Chinese and foreign companies. In the health services exhibition hall, multinational companies such as GE Healthcare and Siemens join hands with leading traditional Chinese medicine institutions, including Tongrentang, a time-honored traditional Chinese medicine store, and the China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences.

Meanwhile, another highlight of this year's session is the strong presence of cutting-edge technologies, as the cultural tourism services section features immersive experiences powered by MR and VR technologies.

CIFTIS presents the latest research outcomes and innovative products from enterprises worldwide, according to a representative from KPMG, adding that the fair has become a major platform for global brands to showcase innovations.

It is learned that this year's fair has seen 198 new products and outcomes released by 113 companies.

On the opening day, China announced that it will keep advancing high-level opening-up in the services sector, accelerating pilot reforms in free trade zones and further improving the business environment.

During the event, many foreign company representatives noted that China's further opening-up has boosted confidence and created new opportunities for global businesses in the services trade. CIFTIS not only helps Chinese services go global, but also brings in international innovations, promoting global exchanges and cooperation. 

Original link: https://en.imsilkroad.com/p/347524.html

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/xinhua-silk-road-2025-ciftis-builds-open-cooperation-platform-injecting-new-impetus-into-global-trade-in-services-302555702.html

SOURCE Xinhua Silk Road

data.title: Trump looks set to delay the TikTok ban again. Why there's no U.S. buyout deal yet.
data.description:
By Victor Reklaitis

The Chinese-controlled app faces a Wednesday deadline for a deal, but it has scored extensions repeatedly

Major trade tensions with China are overshadowing the uncertain U.S. status of TikTok, the Chinese-controlled video platform.

President Donald Trump is expected to give another lifeline to TikTok next week, as his administration's efforts to get the Chinese-controlled app to sell off its U.S. operations appear still to be caught up in broader trade negotiations with Beijing.

Trump has issued three orders directing the U.S. Justice Department not to enforce a bipartisan law that was intended to ban TikTok nationwide as of Jan. 19 as long as the video-sharing platform remains controlled by its Chinese parent company, ByteDance Ltd. His latest order, issued in mid-June, granted a reprieve for TikTok until Sept. 17, which is this coming Wednesday.

Analysts focused on U.S.-China relations are expecting another extension in the coming days, as the Trump administration's main goals with China are achieving a trade deal and preserving a tariff truce that's in place until mid-November.

"I think that Trump will keep extending TikTok's lifeline in the United States until a broader deal can be reached," said Neil Thomas, a fellow at the Asia Society Policy Institute's Center for China Analysis. The president doesn't seem to be in a big rush on TikTok and looks comfortable with the app operating in the U.S., he told MarketWatch.

Thomas also noted that TikTok is among the issues that U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng are expected to talk about when they meet next week in Spain, and it's possible that a deal on the app could come soon.

"Beijing wants to use TikTok as part of its broader leverage in bilateral trade negotiations with the Trump administration and so won't want to give something for nothing," Thomas told MarketWatch. "Getting Beijing's signoff for transferring TikTok's U.S. entity to an American consortium will probably require Washington to give something when it comes to tariff reductions or export controls."

In addition, he said he's looking to see if there's a meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping when they're due to be in South Korea for an Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit, scheduled for Oct. 31-Nov. 1.

The Trump White House launched its own TikTok account in mid-August, and Trump told reporters at that time that security concerns over the app are "highly overrated." The president also said he hadn't spoken to his Chinese counterpart about the app, while emphasizing that another extension was possible, even as there are interested American buyers for TikTok's U.S. business.

"I haven't spoken to President Xi about it. At the right time, when we're set, I'll do it. In the meantime, until the complexity of things work out, we just extend a little bit longer, but we have buyers," Trump said in mid-August.

In April, Trump said that China would have agreed to a deal on a TikTok spinoff if he hadn't announced new tariffs that month.

While a recent report said Trump may be losing patience and could let TikTok go dark briefly, the Hudson Institute's Michael Sobolik said he's in the camp that's expecting another extension in the coming days.

"I'd be pretty surprised if they made TikTok go dark next week, because I'm sure they're frustrated with the delays over TikTok, but it's also no mystery that Trump has 'trade deal' at the top of his priority list," said Sobolik, who is a senior fellow at the conservative think tank.

Sobolik noted that he continues to support the law that has targeted TikTok on national-security grounds, and finds it concerning that the law isn't being enforced.

"Allowing America's foremost geopolitical adversary to control one of the most popular social-media apps in our country is an unacceptable national-security risk," said Sobolik, who is the author of the 2024 book "Countering China's Great Game: A Strategy for American Dominance."

-Victor Reklaitis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-13-25 0800ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: My boyfriend, 69, moved in. He refuses to pay more than $1,300 in rent and won't do yard work. What should I do?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'It's clear that he seems to think that if I die, that he should inherit the house. That is NOT my plan.'

"Last summer, he stopped cutting the grass, so I ended up having to hire someone to periodically do yard maintenance." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

I bought my current home in early 2010. Originally, my boyfriend and I were supposed to go 50/50 on the purchase, but he couldn't sell his old home in time, so everything (deed, mortgage, insurance etc.) is in my name. He did not contribute to the down payment. After we moved into the home, he was offered an early retirement package, which he took, saying he would just take a couple months off and then look for a new position. (He was earning $80,000 a year as a Java programmer).

He agreed to pay a "fair share" in rent and to be responsible for the yardwork and seasonal tasks. Since his retirement income ($11,000 a year) was significantly less than his working income, that amount was originally $900 a month and, when he eventually went back to work, it was supposed to be increased. We share a bedroom. I pay the bills. He pays for his own personal expenses. I am aware that he is now collecting Social Security benefits, plus his pension.

Don't miss: I'm a single parent in my 50s. Should I add my children, 23 and 29, to my mortgage loan to help me qualify?

Increased rent to $1,300

He bumped up his monthly household contribution to $1,300 a month. Last summer he stopped cutting the grass, so I hired someone to do yard maintenance. Last August, I paid $600 for all the yardwork. Yesterday was another $370 (it would have been more, but I supplied the weed block). My boyfriend had offered to chip in no more than $100 a month toward having someone other than him do the yardwork. I suggested that he might need to withdraw some money from his $200,000 IRA. He turns 70 in October.

I sidestepped the inheritance question. It's clear that he seems to think that if I die, he should inherit the house. That is NOT my plan. My plan is for my teenager - who is my adopted daughter and also my grandchild - to inherit the house, and it will be up to her whether my boyfriend remains in the house. I'm not planning to die anytime soon. I'm 63 and in pretty decent health, while my boyfriend is turning 70 and has a stent. Walking more than half a mile wears him out. What should I do? Am I being unreasonable?

Homeowner

Related: 'It's keeping me up at night': My brother sold his share of the family farm and lost the rest in a divorce. He says I owe him.

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

You are two separate entities living under one roof, both attempting to outmaneuver the other.

Dear Homeowner,

You're both reasonable and you're both unreasonable.

The goalposts keep moving at such a rate that it's hard to keep up. He's paying $900 a month. He's getting a job. He's not getting a job. You're charging him $900, plus yardwork and other chores. You're charging him $1,300, but you still expect him to do the yardwork. He's upset that he needs to do yardwork at the age of 69 and pay for the work he doesn't want to do even though it's your house. You're upset that he doesn't buy household goods like detergent and dish soap. This is a years-long domestic tug-of-war. It's a battle of wills. It's a test case in mutual passive aggression.

You both have your own best interests at heart and, given that you are not married, that makes sense. You're both trying to save money for your own retirement and make sure you have as much as possible put away for a rainy day. You are two separate agents living under one roof, each attempting to outmaneuver the other. By the time you have a conversation about household chores or money, so much resentment has built up that it makes a truce nearly impossible. The moment has come to sit down and figure out what you want from each other, emotionally as well as financially.

You can come to an understanding by each setting your own boundaries and expectations, and sticking to them. Of course he was never going to work again. Why would he when he had a place to live and all his bills paid? Of course he was not going to be 100% transparent about his Social Security benefits and pension. Why would he when you are also ignoring your own elephant in the room - namely, the fact that he'll be homeless if you die before he does. You did not marry and, if he's smart, he should have a Plan B. Meanwhile, he is not your Plan A.

Don't miss: My parents promised to split their estate 50/50, but my mother gave my brother real estate. Is that fair?

Cohabitation agreement

This conversation is long overdue. Is he a lover, a gardener, a renter? Are you a girlfriend, a landlady, an employer? It's all so messy: You own the house, he does not. He pays for the privilege of living there. After you raised the rent to $1,300 a month, there was no right or wrong answer as to whether he should continue to do the yardwork. But when the original payment changes, so do all the rules, so you must renegotiate all of the terms. This should be a conversation or series of conversations around the kitchen table, but not one carried out in an ad hoc manner over a period of months or years.

You're both adults. You need to take responsibility for yourselves. You tell him what it is you want and need and he will do the same, and then you negotiate and, hopefully, come to an agreement on the shared bills, rent and household duties. I don't blame him, at nearly 70, for not wanting to work in the garden. And I don't blame him for asking what happens to the house if you die first. You don't need to explain or apologize for leaving the house to your daughter. You just need to tell him the truth. He has a choice: He can remain on mutually agreeable terms, or he can move out.

There are no villains in this story, just two people who need to master the art of clear communication - ideally a signed agreement of cohabitation. This will vary by state. Texas recognizes common-law marriage if a couple agrees to be married, lives together as husband and wife, and represents themselves as married to others, according to the McClure Law Group in Dallas. "Common-law marriages are legally binding in Texas," it says. "A cohabitation agreement can prevent couples who do not intend to enter into a common-law marriage from inadvertently assuming this legal status."

Formalize your living arrangements on paper. And move on.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

I'm 67. My wife, 48, is financially illiterate. How do I teach her to manage our money? After all, I won't be around forever.

'He is increasingly angry': My troubled son lives with me. How do I ensure he is financially secure after I die?

'I am my mother's caregiver': My mom, 93, added my name to her retirement accounts. Will she qualify for Medicaid?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to the Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on the Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-13-25 0708ET

data.title: Schneider Electric Reinforces Commitment to Electrical Contractors with Innovation and Thought Leadership at NECA 2025
data.description:
Schneider Electric Reinforces Commitment to Electrical Contractors with Innovation and Thought Leadership at NECA 2025

Schneider Electric Reinforces Commitment to Electrical Contractors with Innovation and Thought Leadership at NECA 2025

PR Newswire

CHICAGO, Sept. 13, 2025
  • Unveiling new products developed with electrical contractors in mind to meet the quickly evolving needs of the industry
  • Sharing expert insight on the changing 2026 NEC code impacting electrical contractors, their business, and their profitability

CHICAGO, Sept. 13, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Schneider Electric, the leader in the digital transformation of energy management and automation, today showcased innovative new products and led multiple thought leadership discussions at the NECA 2025 Convention & Trade Show, taking place September 12-15 in Chicago.

"For more than 135 years, electrical contractors have relied on solutions and expertise from Schneider Electric to grow their businesses, and we are proud to reinforce our commitment to this critical community at NECA 2025," said Peyton Pratt, Vice President, Power Systems Commercial, Schneider Electric. "By making investments in domestic manufacturing, strengthening our partnerships with leading industry organizations, such as NECA, and focusing on the evolving needs of today's professionals, Schneider Electric is powering the future of electrical contracting."

Building on its history of innovation and trust, Schneider Electric recognizes that the industry's future will be shaped by those willing to learn, evolve, and rebuild at a pace that matches the rapid changes in the environment. The electrical contracting industry is rising to meet this moment, with determination, creativity, and an increasing hunger for collaboration to drive their success. Our presence at NECA 2025 reflects that by providing access to the latest products designed with the contractor in mind, insight into changing regulatory standards impacting the industry, and the support of a true partner investing in today's decision-makers while nurturing a strong bench for tomorrow. 

Innovation on Display in Chicago
Schneider Electric showcased new product innovations developed with the electrical contractor and installer community in mind, including:

LV Reduced Energy Transformer: This three-phase transformer offers a future-ready solution combining efficiency, sustainability, and ease of installation. As one of the first transformers compliant with DOE 2029 regulations, it ensures long-term energy infrastructure versatility, while delivering lower emissions at a higher standard. The smart design simplifies installation, saving time and labor on every project, making it a reliable and forward-thinking choice.

Integrated Power Center 2 (IPC2): The newest addition to the family of Integrated Equipment products, the Integrated Power Center 2 provides maximum flexibility to meet customer specifications and a unique design to reduce their electrical infrastructure footprint. This unique solution allows users to package a LV transformer and power distribution, lighting, and application planes into one prefabricated solutions with all the interconnections done at the factory, reducing on-site labor by up to 90%.

QO Smart Panel Solution: A new modular, flexible solution that adds smart panel functionality easily to standard home electrical systems at a lower cost than current smart panel options. Comprised of Square D™ Control Relays, the Schneider Energy Monitor, and a new or existing Square D™ QO™ Plug-on Neutral electrical panel, this solution delivers smart panel benefits, including app-based circuit-level-control to save energy and enhance safety, and system-wide energy monitoring to better manage energy use and reduce utility bills.

To learn more about these Schneider Electric innovations, or to speak with an expert, please visit the Schneider Electric booth #4618 at the NECA 2025 Convention & Trade Show, September 12-15 in Chicago.

Thought leadership at NECA 2025 
Schneider Electric experts will also be featured speakers in sessions throughout the convention:

Make it American: Exploring Electrical Contractors through Domestic Manufacturing Compliance
Saturday, September 13, 8:00-9:50am: Panel Discussion – MPW Room #W178
This panel will discuss how NEMA's Make It American™ Program can assist contractors in ensuring projects comply with the Build America, Buy America (BABA) requirements, thereby opening doors to new opportunities and markets.

  • Alexa Burr, Vice President of Strategic Growth & Market Development, NEMA (moderator)
  • Julia Hilton, Vice President, Strategy Planning, Schneider Electric
  • Steve Voelzke, President of Electrical Products, Robroy Industries

Preparing for the 2026 NEC Code Changes: What Contractors Need to Know
Sunday, September 14, 2025; 2:00-2:50pm: Speaker Session – Show Floor, level 3 
This session will discuss key updates and revisions to the 2026 National Electrical Code that impact contractors and provide practical guidance on how to comply with the new requirements. 

  • Chad Kennedy, Director, Industry Standards, Schneider Electric
  • Kyle Krueger, Executive Director, Codes and Standards, NECA

Veterans Recruitment Initiative
Monday, September 15, 2025; 8:00am-8:50am: Convention Education – MPW Room #W185BC
This session will discuss new initiatives NECA is undertaking to recruit the over 200,000 Veterans transitioning out of the military and re-entering the civilian job market annually to our industry through VEEP, VIPER, and our coalition of electrical associations.

  • Ron Bailey, Vice President, Industry Development, NECA (moderator)
  • Brandy Malone, Federal Policy Manager, Schneider Electric
  • Melissa West, Executive Director, Field Services and Strategic Leadership Initiatives, NECA
  • Greg Long, President, Long Electric Company
  • Andy Berg, Executive Director, NECA San Diego Chapter

For more information about Schneider Electric and our innovative products, tools, and programs developed to support the Electrical Contractor community, please visit us online at www.se.com/us/contractors. 

About Schneider Electric

Schneider's purpose is to create Impact by empowering all to make the most of our energy and resources, bridging progress and sustainability. At Schneider, we call this Life Is On.

Our mission is to be the trusted partner in Sustainability and Efficiency.

We are a global industrial technology leader bringing world-leading expertise in electrification, automation and digitalization to smart industries, resilient infrastructure, future-proof data centers, intelligent buildings, and intuitive homes. Anchored by our deep domain expertise, we provide integrated end-to-end lifecycle AI enabled Industrial IoT solutions with connected products, automation, software and services, delivering digital twins to enable profitable growth for our customers.

We are a people company with an ecosystem of 160,000 colleagues and more than a million partners operating in over 100 countries to ensure proximity to our customers and stakeholders.

www.se.com  

Discover Life Is On

data.title: 'It's keeping me up at night': My brother sold his share of the family farm and lost the rest in a divorce. He says I owe him.
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'He told me that he will legally pursue me and my farm through the court'

"I'm 46 and our daughter is getting married this fall, but I'm so worried that I can't look forward to this happy time." (Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

Nine years ago, my brother and I lost our father. We grew up on a non-working farm where most of the land had been leased to other surrounding farms. When our father passed away, we received a rough 50/50 split. I attempted to use my half as best I could as a dairy farm and was successful.

My brother, on the other hand, sold most of his share and lost the other through a divorce. Now my brother wants in on my part. He wants a stake in the land and the farm, saying that our dad would want us to work together and that the farm is his "right." He uses phrases like, "Dad would be turning in his grave."

The latest development: He has mentioned a lawyer. He told me that he will legally pursue me and my farm through the court. Does he have a case? Will I lose my farming business? I'm really worried, and it's keeping me up at night. I'm 46 and our daughter is getting married this fall, but I'm so worried that I can't look forward to this happy time.

The Brother

Related: I'm about to inherit a lot of money. How do I make sure my husband doesn't get any of it?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

In legal parlance, this would be a "frivolous" lawsuit.

Dear Brother,

You're not responsible for your brother's decisions. He does not have a case. You will not lose your farming business.

He can spend money on lawyers, but I doubt he will find a reputable attorney who would take his case. What are his grievances? Your father left you a 50/50 ownership in a farm. He chose to sell a portion of his share, and he lost the other portion in a divorce - and now you owe him? The question the lawyer will ask is, "Why?" Because you were always the favorite growing up? Because you were always the sensible one? Because you stood by and watched while your brother made a series of unfortunate decisions? That's when your brother's attorney should say, "You don't have any legal grounds."

In legal parlance, this would be a "frivolous" lawsuit. "A frivolous lawsuit is a lawsuit filed by an attorney who knowingly advances a case despite a lack of legal precedent, facts, or merit," according to Babcock Tucker, a law firm based in Denver. "It might be filed for ulterior motives, such as to harass or coerce someone, rather than to resolve a legitimate legal grievance. Courts can dismiss these cases early in the process and may impose fines, sanctions, and other penalties on the parties or attorneys who engage in such conduct."

Nuisance lawsuits are often used to harass or intimidate.

Your brother wants to sue you for something he did - court cases are based on the opposite being true. Nuisance lawsuits are often used to harass or intimidate and/or when the plaintiff alleges injuries or damages that are either nonexistent or grossly exaggerated beyond the scope of reality, the law firm adds. "These are cases filed to harass or burden the defendant rather than to resolve a legitimate legal grievance. They can be used as a tactic to intimidate, coerce, or extract settlements from the target under the threat of legal fees and court costs."

He is threatening to sue you for emotional reasons, not legal ones. So put your mind at rest, and don't allow this to spoil your daughter's marriage. It's hard to detach from a person, particularly a family member, who is making their problems your problems. In pop-psychology, he would be referred to as a "crazy maker." After you exchange letters from your respective lawyers - if it escalates to that level, I suggest you communicate through a lawyer - meet him with a wall of silence. He's your brother, but it doesn't help if you allow him to create so much discord in your life.

The other elephant in the room here is grief and recrimination. The loss of a parent, especially someone who held sway over the family and kept order both financially and emotionally, can unleash a lot of long-hidden resentments and even enable adult children to revert to their formative years when they may have acted out. That seems to be what your brother is doing here. If he had genuine legal standing, his threats would make more sense. But this seems like his way of airing older grievances.

Your brother will have an uphill challenge finding a lawyer, as taking on such a frivolous lawsuit could harm their reputation. Please enjoy your daughter's wedding.

Related: My mother, 81, discovered her 'millions' in investments are gone. What can I do?

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

My friend, 61, signed a $20K contract for dental implants. She can't afford it. Can she fight it?

My mom, 93, added my name to her bank and retirement accounts. Will she qualify for Medicaid?

My friend married for a second time. Will the kids from her first marriage lose their inheritance?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to the Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on the Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-13-25 0631ET

data.title: SKY Perfect Modernizes Playout-to-Delivery with Harmonic
data.description:
SKY Perfect Modernizes Playout-to-Delivery with Harmonic

SKY Perfect Modernizes Playout-to-Delivery with Harmonic

PR Newswire

SAN JOSE, Calif., Sept. 13, 2025

Harmonic's Software-Based XOS Advanced Media Processor Provides
Unparalleled Efficiency and Unlocks New Business Models  

SAN JOSE, Calif., Sept. 13, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Harmonic (NASDAQ: HLIT) today announced that SKY Perfect JSAT Corporation (SJC), a leading satellite operator and pay-TV provider in Japan, has partnered with Harmonic to modernize its broadcast playout and delivery workflow. Leveraging Harmonic's award-winning software-based XOS Advanced Media Processor, SJC can cost-effectively deliver exceptional video quality to viewers and tap into new business models to drive revenue growth.

"Streamlining our playout-and-delivery workflow with Harmonic's software solutions is the next step in our long-standing collaboration with the company that is built on a foundation of proven performance and outstanding support," said Takashi Maenaka, team leader of the media technology unit at SKY Perfect JSAT Corporation. "The flexibility of Harmonic's XOS media processor positions us to expand into new market segments and launch targeted services, paving the way for sustained future growth."

Harmonic's XOS media processor features a unique cloud-native software foundation, a complete playout-to-delivery feature set and next-generation media processing powered by the PURE Compression Engine™. By consolidating critical broadcast functions into a single solution, the XOS media processor enhances SJC's operational efficiency, delivers substantial cost savings and provides a 40% improvement in density compared with the previous platform, driving performance gains.

"SKY Perfect JSAT is unlocking unprecedented efficiency by strategically investing in software-centric solutions," said Tony Berthaud, senior vice president, sales, APAC and EMEA at Harmonic. "Leveraging cutting-edge compression and playout capabilities powered by the Harmonic XOS media processor, SJC can deliver exceptional viewing experiences and thrive in the rapidly evolving broadcast landscape."

Harmonic will showcase the latest innovations to its XOS advanced media processor at IBC2025, Sept. 12-15 in booth 1.B20. To schedule a meeting with the company, visit www.harmonicinc.com/video-streaming/events/ibc. More information about Harmonic's solutions is available at www.harmonicinc.com.

About Harmonic
Harmonic (NASDAQ: HLIT), the worldwide leader in virtualized broadband and video delivery solutions, enables media companies and service providers to deliver ultra-high-quality video streaming and broadcast services to consumers globally. The company revolutionized broadband networking via the industry's first virtualized broadband solution, enabling operators to more flexibly deploy gigabit internet services to consumers' homes and mobile devices. Whether simplifying OTT video delivery via innovative cloud and software platforms, or powering the delivery of gigabit internet services, Harmonic is changing the way media companies and service providers monetize live and on-demand content on every screen. More information is available at www.harmonicinc.com. 

Legal Notice Regarding Forward-Looking Statements
This press release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933 and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Statements concerning Harmonic's business and the anticipated capabilities, advantages, reliability, efficiency, market acceptance, market growth, specifications and benefits of Harmonic products, services and technology are forward-looking statements. These statements are based on our current expectations and beliefs and are subject to risks and uncertainties, including the risks and uncertainties more fully described in Harmonic's filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission, including its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended Dec. 31, 2024, its Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and its Current Reports on Form 8-K. The forward-looking statements in this press release are based on information available to Harmonic as of the date hereof, and Harmonic disclaims any obligation to update any forward-looking statements.

Harmonic, the Harmonic logo and other Harmonic marks are owned by Harmonic Inc. or its affiliates. All other trademarks referenced herein are the property of their respective owners.

data.title: Hacienda La Puente Unified School District Provides Notification of a Data Security Incident
data.description:
Hacienda La Puente Unified School District Provides Notification of a Data Security Incident

Hacienda La Puente Unified School District Provides Notification of a Data Security Incident

PR Newswire

September 13

City of Industry, CA – Hacienda La Puente Unified School District ("HLPUSD") has become aware of a data security incident that may have impacted personal information belonging to certain individuals. HLPUSD is not aware of any evidence to suggest that any information has been misused. 

On April 12, 2024, HLPUSD discovered it was victimized by a sophisticated ransomware attack. Upon discovery, HLPUSD immediately began working with its I.T. team and third-party forensic specialists to secure the network, restore its systems to operability, and investigate the full scope and nature of the incident. HLPUSD also reported this incident to federal law enforcement and the California Cybersecurity Integration Center. Through the investigation, HLPUSD learned that its systems were subject to unauthorized access from February 9, 2024 to April 12, 2024. HLPUSD also determined that certain files may have been subject to unauthorized access during the incident. As a result, with the assistance of third-party specialists, HLPUSD began a comprehensive and time-intensive review process to identify what type of information may have been contained within the potentially impacted files, and to whom that information belonged.

Following a comprehensive and time-intensive analysis, HLPUSD determined that information contained in the affected files may have included personally identifiable information ("PII"). The type of information contained within the affected data may include a first and last name, in combination with one or more of the following: Social Security number, driver's license or state identification number, health insurance information, medical diagnosis or treatment information, and/or username or email in combination with a password.

At this time, HLPUSD is not aware of any evidence to suggest that any information has been fraudulently misused. However, in an abundance of caution, HLPUSD is notifying potentially impacted individuals of this incident. Although HLPUSD has no reason to believe any individual's information has been or will be misused as a result of this incident, individuals are nonetheless encouraged to monitor their account statements for suspicious activity and to detect errors.

HLPUSD has established a toll-free number to answer questions about the incident and to address related concerns. The number to call is 1-800-939-4170, from Monday through Friday, 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. Pacific Time. 

HLPUSD takes the privacy and security of the information in its care seriously, and sincerely regrets any worry or inconvenience this incident may have caused.

Media Contact:
Caitlin Lawlor, Esq.
clawlor@c-wlaw.com 

/PRNewswire -- Sept. 13, 2025/

SOURCE Hacienda La Puente Unified School District

data.title: UFCW 8-Golden State Reaches Tentative Agreement with Raley’s, Bel Air, and Nob Hill—Thousands of Grocery Workers to Benefit
data.description:
UFCW 8-Golden State Reaches Tentative Agreement with Raley’s, Bel Air, and Nob Hill—Thousands of Grocery Workers to Benefit

UFCW 8-Golden State reached a tentative agreement with Raley’s, covering thousands of members at Raley’s, Bel Air, and Nob Hill stores across Central and Northern California. The proposed contract includes significant wage increases, ratification bonuses, and strengthened health care and retirement benefits, along with improved scheduling and job protections.

“This agreement is the direct result of members remaining united throughout the process,” said UFCW 8-Golden State President Jacques Loveall. “Our members don’t just keep these stores running, they’re the heart of their communities. This agreement honors their dedication and ensures they’re treated with the respect they deserve.”

The new three-and-a-half-year tentative agreement includes:

  • Wage increases of $4.00 per hour over three and a half years for Experienced Clerks and above, and Journeyman Meat Cutters
  • Additional wage increases for many in the step progressions, effective after ratification
  • Contract ratification bonuses of $1,100 for full-time employees and $750 for part-time employees
  • Increased Sunday premium pay for eligible employees with 20 or more years of service
  • Enhanced premium pay for Managing Clerk, Sr. Head Clerk, Sr. Produce Clerk, and Night Crew Manager positions
  • Increased funding to the Lifetime Income Security Account (LISA) Pension Plan, ensuring a fortified and secure retirement
  • Fully funded health care benefits and improved sick leave payout provisions
  • Strengthened language around scheduling, seniority holidays, bereavement leave, and job protection
  • Contract term extending through October 14, 2028

“We’re deeply grateful for the continuous support of the members. Their unwavering commitment has a meaningful impact in negotiations,” Loveall added. “We also recognize the constructive dialogue with the employer which helped bring this agreement to the table.”

Contract ratification materials are currently being prepared. Members will have the opportunity to review the full details of the tentative agreement and speak with their District Union Representative before voting. Information about the voting process will be shared with members in the coming days.

Together we have proven once again Solidarity Works!

Jacques Loveall: JSLoveall@ufcw8.org

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250912701158/en/
